title: ICMA Green Bond Principles (GBP) 2021
description: The Green Bond Principles are voluntary process guidelines for issuing green bonds, published by the International Capital Market Association. They promote integrity in the green bond market through transparency, disclosure, and reporting.

fourCorePillars: Process for Project Evaluation and Selection
eligibleCategories: Environmentally sustainable management of living natural resources and land use
Circular economy adapted products, production technologies and processes

reportingRequirements.allocationReporting: Annual until full allocation, and on a timely basis in case of material developments. Should include list of projects, amounts allocated, and expected impact.
reportingRequirements.impactReporting: Recommended. Quantitative performance measures such as GHG emissions reduced/avoided, renewable energy generated, energy efficiency gained.
reportingRequirements.externalReview: Recommended but not mandatory. Types include second party opinion, verification, certification, and green bond scoring/rating.

title: Climate Bonds Standard v4.0
description: The Climate Bonds Standard provides a science-based certification scheme for green bonds and loans, ensuring alignment with Paris Agreement goals. Version 4.0 introduced transition requirements and enhanced social safeguards.

steps: Pre-issuance: Issuer engages approved verifier to confirm alignment with sector criteria
Issuance: Bond is issued with Climate Bonds Certification mark
Post-issuance: Annual reporting and verification within 24 months of issuance
certificationProcess.cost: Certification fee of 0.1 basis points of issuance value (minimum USD 2,500)

energy.solar: Eligible. Must demonstrate lifecycle emissions below 100 gCO2e/kWh
energy.geothermal: Eligible if lifecycle emissions below 100 gCO2e/kWh

transport.rail: Electric rail eligible; diesel with transition pathway

buildings.residential: Top 15% of local market or major renovation achieving 30% energy improvement
buildings.commercial: Top 15% of local market by carbon intensity

sectorCriteria.water: Infrastructure for supply, treatment, flood defense, and nature-based solutions
sectorCriteria.waste: Recycling, composting, waste-to-energy with efficiency thresholds

transitionRequirements.entityLevel: Issuer must have a Paris-aligned transition plan
transitionRequirements.disclosures: Scope 1, 2, and material Scope 3 GHG emissions
transitionRequirements.lobbyingAlignment: Climate lobbying activities must be consistent with Paris goals

title: GCC Green Bond and Sukuk Issuances
description: Major green bond and sustainable sukuk issuances from GCC-based entities, reflecting the region's growing commitment to sustainable finance alongside its energy transition strategies.

issuances.note: Largest green sukuk from a GCC energy company

title: Global Green Bond Market Size 2020-2024
description: Annual green bond issuance volumes by region, showing the rapid growth of the green bond market from USD 297 billion in 2020 to an estimated USD 575 billion in 2024.

title: Green Sukuk Framework and Sharia-Compliance Requirements
description: Green sukuk combine Islamic finance principles with green bond standards, requiring both Sharia compliance and environmental use-of-proceeds alignment. The structure avoids interest (riba) and ensures asset-backing.

data.definition: A Sharia-compliant financial certificate (sukuk) where proceeds are exclusively applied to finance or refinance eligible green projects, aligned with ICMA Green Bond Principles and Sharia requirements.

shariaRequirements.assetBacking: Sukuk must be backed by tangible assets, usufruct, or services — not purely financial claims
shariaRequirements.profitLossSharing: Returns linked to asset performance rather than fixed interest rates
shariaRequirements.shariaBoard: Independent Sharia Supervisory Board must approve structure and ongoing compliance

commonStructures.name: Ijara (lease-based)
commonStructures.description: SPV purchases green assets and leases them to the originator. Rental payments fund coupon distribution. Well-suited for renewable energy assets and green buildings.

commonStructures.name: Wakala (agency)
commonStructures.description: SPV appoints originator as agent to invest proceeds in a portfolio of green assets. Agent earns incentive fee for returns exceeding agreed benchmark.

commonStructures.name: Murabaha (cost-plus)
commonStructures.description: SPV purchases green commodities/assets and sells to originator at a disclosed markup payable in installments.

keyMarketMilestones.event: First green sukuk issued by Tadau Energy (Malaysia), MYR 250 million for solar project
Republic of Indonesia issued first sovereign green sukuk, USD 1.25 billion
Islamic Development Bank (IsDB) issued USD 1.5 billion Sustainability Sukuk
Saudi Arabia issued first green sukuk under Vision 2030
Aramco issued USD 6 billion green sukuk, largest from GCC energy sector

regulatoryFrameworks.malaysia: Securities Commission Malaysia Green SRI Sukuk Framework (2014, updated 2019)
regulatoryFrameworks.indonesia: OJK Green Bond and Green Sukuk Regulation (2017)
regulatoryFrameworks.gcc: No unified GCC green sukuk standard; individual country frameworks apply
regulatoryFrameworks.international: IIFM Sukuk standards combined with ICMA GBP and CBI Standard

title: MSCI ESG Ratings Methodology
description: MSCI ESG Ratings measure a company's resilience to long-term ESG risks on a AAA to CCC scale. The methodology uses a rules-based approach with 35 key issues weighted by industry, adjusted for company-specific exposure and management.

process: Identify key issues relevant to each GICS sub-industry
Assign weights based on impact and timeline of risk materialization
Score companies 0-10 on each key issue based on exposure and management
Weight and aggregate scores; normalize within industry peer group
scoringMethodology.dataSources: Company disclosures, government databases, NGO data, media screening, 1,600+ data points per company
scoringMethodology.coverageUniverse: Approximately 8,500 companies and 680,000+ equity and fixed income securities globally

title: S&P Global Corporate Sustainability Assessment (CSA)
description: The S&P Global CSA is an annual evaluation of companies' sustainability practices that feeds into the Dow Jones Sustainability Indices. It uses a detailed questionnaire-based approach with industry-specific criteria and weightings.

methodology.approach: Annual questionnaire sent to companies (participation voluntary); non-participants assessed using publicly available information

scoringApproach.scoring: Questions scored from 0 to 1, weighted and aggregated to dimension and total scores
scoringApproach.mediaAndStakeholderAnalysis: Negative screening component can reduce scores based on controversies
scoringApproach.percentileRanking: Companies ranked within their industry using percentile scores

data.coverage: Over 13,000 companies assessed (2024), approximately 3,500 participating directly

title: Sustainalytics ESG Risk Ratings Methodology
description: Sustainalytics ESG Risk Ratings measure a company's exposure to industry-specific material ESG issues and how well it manages those risks. The framework focuses on unmanaged risk, scored on a 0-100 scale where lower is better.

coreComponents.name: Exposure
coreComponents.description: Company's vulnerability to material ESG issues at the subindustry and company-specific level
factors: Company-specific exposure adjustments based on business model, geography, products

coreComponents.name: Management
coreComponents.description: How well a company manages its ESG risks through policies, programs, and performance

coreComponents.name: Unmanaged Risk
coreComponents.description: Residual ESG risk after accounting for management efforts. This is the headline rating.
coreComponents.formula: Unmanaged Risk = Exposure - (Manageable Risk * Management Score)

data.updateFrequency: Full re-rating annually; continuous monitoring for controversies

title: CDP Scoring Methodology
description: CDP (formerly Carbon Disclosure Project) scores companies, cities, states, and regions on their environmental disclosure and action across climate change, forests, and water security questionnaires.

levels.label: Management
levels.description: Taking coordinated action on environmental issues

levels.label: Failure
levels.description: Failed to provide sufficient information or did not respond

questionnaires.name: Climate Change
questionnaires.weightingApproach: Points allocated per question based on environmental impact significance, aggregated and normalized

title: GRESB Real Estate and Infrastructure Assessment
description: GRESB provides ESG benchmarks for real assets, covering real estate and infrastructure portfolios. It is the leading global ESG benchmark for real estate and infrastructure investments, used by over 170 institutional investors.

scoring.starRating: 1-5 stars based on quintile ranking relative to peers
scoring.greenStarDesignation: Score >= 50 in both Management and Performance components
scoring.sectorLeader: Top entity in each sector group based on GRESB Score and public disclosure

title: ESG Rating Provider Methodology Overview: ISS ESG, FTSE4Good, Moody's ESG
description: Overview of methodology for three additional major ESG rating providers, covering ISS ESG Corporate Rating, FTSE Russell ESG Ratings (FTSE4Good), and Moody's ESG Score (formerly Vigeo Eiris).

providers.name: ISS ESG Corporate Rating
providers.methodology: Over 100 industry-specific ESG indicators. Two dimensions: Transparency Rating (disclosure quality) and Performance Rating (ESG management quality). Industry-specific key issues mapped to SDGs.
providers.updateFrequency: Annual full update; continuous controversy monitoring

providers.name: FTSE Russell ESG Ratings (FTSE4Good Index Series)
providers.methodology: 14 ESG themes with over 300 indicators. Scores on exposure (0-3) and practice/performance (0-5) per theme. Industry-specific materiality matrix determines which themes apply.
providers.coverage: Over 8,000 securities across 47 developed and emerging markets
providers.updateFrequency: Semi-annual review for index inclusion; continuous data updates

providers.name: Moody's ESG Score
providers.methodology: 38 ESG criteria organized into 6 domains: Environment, Human Resources, Human Rights, Community Involvement, Business Behavior, Corporate Governance. Sector-specific weighting applied.
keyFeatures: Climate risk physical and transition scoring

title: EU Taxonomy: Environmental Objectives and Key Economic Activities
description: The EU Taxonomy is a classification system establishing a list of environmentally sustainable economic activities. It provides technical screening criteria for determining whether an activity makes a substantial contribution to one of six environmental objectives while doing no significant harm to the others.

environmentalObjectives.name: Sustainable use and protection of water and marine resources
Protection and restoration of biodiversity and ecosystems

topEconomicActivities.activity: Electricity generation using solar photovoltaic technology
topEconomicActivities.criteria: Direct emissions from generation must be below 100 gCO2e/kWh
Lifecycle GHG emissions below 100 gCO2e/kWh
Power density >5 W/m2 or lifecycle emissions <100 gCO2e/kWh
Primary energy demand at least 10% below NZEB threshold; EPC class A where applicable
At least 30% reduction in primary energy demand
Zero direct (tailpipe) CO2 emissions
Zero direct CO2 emissions or trains with zero direct emissions on tracks with necessary infrastructure
Zero direct emissions for passenger cars/LCVs; heavy-duty vehicles per CO2 standards
Lifecycle GHG emissions below 3 tCO2e/tH2
System uses at least 50% renewable energy, 50% waste heat, 75% CHP, or 50% combination thereof
Energy efficiency of system, leakage rates, water quality monitoring
Net energy consumption <= threshold; treated water meets quality standards
Nature-based solutions preferred; climate risk assessment required

topEconomicActivities.activity: Collection and transport of non-hazardous waste
topEconomicActivities.criteria: Separately collected for reuse/recycling; waste is not landfilled/incinerated
At least 50% of processed waste converted into secondary raw materials by weight

topEconomicActivities.activity: Data-driven solutions for GHG emissions reductions
topEconomicActivities.criteria: ICT solutions that enable GHG emission reductions in other sectors

topEconomicActivities.activity: Non-life insurance: underwriting of climate-related perils
topEconomicActivities.criteria: Products that promote climate adaptation
Forest management plan; LULUCF requirements; biodiversity safeguards
Adaptation plan, soil conservation, water efficiency

topEconomicActivities.activity: Engineering and technical consultancy for energy efficiency
topEconomicActivities.criteria: Services directly enabling energy efficiency improvements

data.dnshCriteria: Each activity must demonstrate Do No Significant Harm (DNSH) to remaining five objectives
data.minimumSafeguards: Alignment with OECD Guidelines for MNEs, UN Guiding Principles on Business and Human Rights, ILO Core Conventions

title: ASEAN Taxonomy for Sustainable Finance v3
description: The ASEAN Taxonomy for Sustainable Finance provides a common language for sustainable finance classification across ASEAN member states, featuring a multi-tiered assessment approach that accommodates varying levels of development.

foundationFramework.description: Principles-based approach using essential criteria for quick classification

plusStandard.description: Technical screening criteria with quantitative thresholds for detailed activity-level assessment
plusStandard.assessmentBasis: Quantitative metrics aligned with science-based targets

environmentalObjectives: Protection of healthy ecosystems and biodiversity
Promotion of resource resilience and transition to circular economy

trafficLightClassification.green: Activities making substantial contribution to at least one environmental objective
trafficLightClassification.amber: Activities not yet aligned with green thresholds but on a transition pathway with a time-bound plan to achieve green thresholds
trafficLightClassification.red: Activities causing significant harm to environmental objectives with no credible transition plan

focusSectors: Electricity, Gas, Steam and Air Conditioning Supply
Water, Sewerage, Waste Management and Remediation
data.transitionCategories: Recognizes transition activities for hard-to-abate sectors with time-bound improvement pathways; unique feature accommodating developing economy realities

source: Saudi National Center for Privatization (NCP) / CMA
title: Saudi Arabia Green Taxonomy Development Status
description: Saudi Arabia is developing a national green taxonomy as part of the Saudi Green Initiative and Vision 2030. As of 2024, the taxonomy remains in development/consultation phase, with alignment expected to the Common Ground Taxonomy approach.

prioritySectors: Green buildings and sustainable real estate

context.saudiGreenInitiative: Announced 2021: targets 50% renewable energy by 2030, net zero by 2060, planting 10 billion trees
context.visionAlignment: Supports Saudi Vision 2030 economic diversification goals
context.uniqueConsiderations: Must accommodate hydrocarbon transition pathways given oil-dependent economy; includes blue hydrogen and CCUS as eligible activities

data.timeline: Draft expected for public consultation by 2025; final version anticipated 2026

source: UK Government / Green Technical Advisory Group
title: UK Green Taxonomy Consultation Status
description: The UK announced development of its own green taxonomy following Brexit. The Green Technical Advisory Group (GTAG) published advice in 2022, but formal adoption has been delayed multiple times.

data.status: Consultation and review phase; adoption delayed

timeline.2021: UK Government announced plans for UK Green Taxonomy
timeline.2022: GTAG published advice on UK Taxonomy design; consultation on first two objectives
timeline.2023: Government delayed formal consultation; review of international approaches ongoing
timeline.2024: Revised consultation expected; alignment with ISSB standards under consideration
timeline.expected: Formal adoption timeline uncertain; dependent on Sustainability Disclosure Standards implementation

proposedObjectives: Protecting and enhancing the natural environment (biodiversity, water, marine)
Promoting resource efficiency and the circular economy
keyDifferencesFromEU: Potential for nuclear to be included as eligible from outset
Alignment with UK Net Zero Strategy rather than EU Fit for 55
Coordination with UK Sustainability Disclosure Standards (endorsement of ISSB)
data.gtagRecommendations: Use science-based thresholds; adopt activity-level approach; include transition activities; ensure interoperability with EU Taxonomy where possible

title: Common Ground Taxonomy: EU-China Comparison
description: The Common Ground Taxonomy (CGT), developed by the IPSF, compares the EU Taxonomy and China's Green Bond Endorsed Project Catalogue to identify commonalities, enabling cross-border green finance flows.

data.purpose: Identify common elements between EU and China taxonomies to facilitate cross-border green investment and reduce fragmentation

structuralDifferences.euTaxonomy: Activity-level classification with technical screening criteria, DNSH, and minimum safeguards
structuralDifferences.chinaCatalogue: Project-level classification in China's Green Bond Endorsed Project Catalogue (2021 edition); less granular screening criteria

keyDifferences: Nuclear energy: Included in China catalogue; included in EU taxonomy as transitional under specific conditions
Natural gas: Included in China catalogue without conditions; included in EU taxonomy as transitional with strict criteria
Coal: Clean coal included in China catalogue (phasing out); excluded from EU taxonomy
DNSH: EU requires do-no-significant-harm assessment; China catalogue does not have equivalent requirement

data.participants: EU, China, and observers including Argentina, Canada, Chile, India, Indonesia, Japan, Kenya, Malaysia, Morocco, New Zealand, Norway, Senegal, Singapore, Switzerland, UK
data.impact: CGT facilitates mutual recognition and interoperability, reducing costs for issuers operating in both markets

title: EU Taxonomy Alignment Calculation: Revenue, CapEx, and OpEx Methodology
description: Under the EU Taxonomy Regulation, financial and non-financial undertakings must disclose the proportion of their activities that are taxonomy-aligned based on three key performance indicators: revenue, capital expenditure (CapEx), and operating expenditure (OpEx).

turnover.denominator: Total net revenue (as per IFRS or applicable GAAP)
turnover.purpose: Shows proportion of current business that is environmentally sustainable

capex.numerator: Capital expenditure related to assets or processes associated with taxonomy-aligned activities, plus CapEx that is part of a plan to expand or achieve taxonomy-aligned activities within 5 years
capex.denominator: Total additions to tangible and intangible assets during the financial year (IAS 16, IAS 38, IFRS 16)
capex.purpose: Shows investment direction toward sustainability transition

opex.numerator: Operating expenditure related to taxonomy-aligned activities: non-capitalised R&D, short-term leases, building renovation, maintenance and repair
opex.denominator: Total direct non-capitalised costs related to R&D, building renovation, short-term leases, maintenance and repair
opex.purpose: Shows day-to-day expenditure on taxonomy-aligned activities

alignmentTest.step1: Identify eligible economic activities (those covered by the taxonomy)
alignmentTest.step2: Assess substantial contribution: does the activity meet technical screening criteria for at least one environmental objective?
alignmentTest.step3: Assess DNSH: does the activity do no significant harm to the other five environmental objectives?
alignmentTest.step4: Verify minimum safeguards: alignment with OECD Guidelines, UNGPs, ILO conventions
alignmentTest.step5: Calculate the proportion (%) of aligned turnover/CapEx/OpEx

disclosureRequirements.nonFinancialCompanies: Disclose all three KPIs under NFRD/CSRD

financialInstitutions.banks: Green Asset Ratio (GAR) — proportion of balance sheet in taxonomy-aligned activities
financialInstitutions.assetManagers: Proportion of AUM invested in taxonomy-aligned activities

title: PCAF Global GHG Accounting and Reporting Standard v2 (Financed Emissions)
description: The Partnership for Carbon Accounting Financials (PCAF) Standard provides a methodology for financial institutions to measure and disclose the GHG emissions associated with their loans and investments (Scope 3, Category 15).

data.attributionApproach: Attribution factor represents the financial institution's share of total financing in the borrower or investee

listedEquityAndCorporateBonds.formula: Attribution Factor = Outstanding amount / (Enterprise Value Including Cash, EVIC)
listedEquityAndCorporateBonds.financedEmissions: Sum of (Attribution Factor * Company Emissions) for each investee

businessLoans.formula: Attribution Factor = Outstanding amount / Total equity + debt of the borrower
businessLoans.note: When EVIC not available, use total balance sheet equity + debt

projectFinance.formula: Attribution Factor = Outstanding amount / Total project equity + debt
projectFinance.note: For project-level emissions; Scope 1 and 2 of the project

commercialRealEstate.formula: Attribution Factor = Outstanding amount / Property value at origination
commercialRealEstate.note: Emissions based on building energy performance

mortgages.formula: Attribution Factor = Outstanding amount / Property value at origination
mortgages.note: Emissions based on building energy performance data (EPC, estimated)

motorVehicles.formula: Attribution Factor = Outstanding amount / Vehicle value at origination
motorVehicles.note: Emissions based on vehicle type, fuel efficiency, average annual distance

dataQualityScores.description: Audited GHG emissions data or actual primary energy consumption data from the borrower/investee
Non-audited GHG emissions data, or actual energy consumption data from the borrower/investee
Emissions estimated using physical activity data (e.g., production data) from the borrower/investee, combined with verified emission factors
Emissions estimated using economic activity data (e.g., revenue, assets) from the borrower/investee
Emissions estimated using proxy data based on sector averages or regional averages, without borrower-specific data

data.signatories: Over 440 financial institutions with USD 90+ trillion in assets (2024)

title: PCAF Financed Emissions Calculation by Asset Class
description: Detailed calculation methodologies for each PCAF asset class, including formulas, data requirements, and emission factor selection guidance.

listedEquityAndCorporateBonds.emissionsScope: Scope 1 and Scope 2 (Scope 3 recommended where material)
listedEquityAndCorporateBonds.evicDefinition: Market capitalization + total debt (including current portion of long-term debt)

businessLoansAndUnlistedEquity.emissionsScope: Scope 1 and Scope 2 (Scope 3 for high-impact sectors)
businessLoansAndUnlistedEquity.note: For unlisted companies, use total balance sheet equity + total outstanding debt as denominator

projectFinance.note: For construction phase, use estimated annual operational emissions

commercialRealEstate.emissionsScope: Scope 1 (on-site combustion) and Scope 2 (purchased energy) of the building
commercialRealEstate.dataPreference: Actual energy consumption > EPC-based estimates > floor area benchmarks

mortgages.emissionsScope: Scope 1 and 2 of the residential property
mortgages.dataPreference: EPC label > national average by building type and vintage > regional/national proxies

motorVehicleLoans.emissionFactors: Vehicle type, fuel type, manufacturer efficiency data, average annual km driven

title: Net-Zero Banking Alliance (NZBA) Target-Setting Guidelines
description: The NZBA requires member banks to set intermediate (2030) and long-term (2050) targets for priority sectors, aligning financing portfolios with pathways to net-zero by 2050. Members commit to transparent reporting on progress.

commitments.overarchingGoal: Align operational and attributable GHG emissions from lending and investment portfolios with pathways to net-zero by 2050
commitments.intermediateTargets: Set 2030 (or sooner) targets for priority sectors within 18 months of joining
commitments.sectorCoverage: Targets must cover a significant majority of financed emissions, prioritizing carbon-intensive sectors

metrics: Physical emission intensity (e.g., tCO2e/MWh, tCO2e/t steel)
targetSettingGuidelines.scenarios: Must use credible, science-based no/low-overshoot 1.5C scenarios (e.g., IEA NZE2050, NGFS Net Zero 2050)
targetSettingGuidelines.coverage: Targets must cover a significant majority of the bank's financed emissions in each priority sector
targetSettingGuidelines.scope: Scope 1, 2, and material Scope 3 emissions of clients (not the bank's own operational emissions)

reportingRequirements.annualDisclosure: Progress against targets for each sector, absolute emissions and intensities, methodology used
reportingRequirements.transitionPlans: Publish bank-level transition strategy within 18 months of joining

title: Glasgow Financial Alliance for Net Zero (GFANZ) Progress and Gaps
description: GFANZ is a global coalition of financial institutions committed to accelerating the decarbonization of the economy. Launched at COP26 in 2021, it coordinates net-zero commitments across banking, asset management, insurance, and other financial sub-sectors.

overview.convener: Mark Carney (UN Special Envoy for Climate Action and Finance)

sectorAlliances.name: Net Zero Financial Service Providers Alliance (NZFSPA)

commitments.totalAssets: USD 130+ trillion at peak (2022); reduced as some members withdrew
commitments.coreCommitment: Members commit to net-zero GHG emissions by 2050 from all operational and financed emissions
commitments.transitionPlans: Members must publish credible, science-based transition plans

progressMetrics.targetSetting: Approximately 60% of NZBA members published 2030 sector targets by end of 2023
progressMetrics.transitionPlans: Growing number of published plans, but quality and comprehensiveness vary widely
progressMetrics.engagementPolicies: Most members have climate engagement policies for high-emitting clients

gaps: Significant member attrition, particularly in NZIA (antitrust concerns in US)
Inconsistency in target-setting methodologies across alliances
Limited accountability mechanisms for members failing to meet commitments
Fossil fuel phase-out policies remain rare; most members still finance oil and gas expansion
Scope 3 financed emissions measurement remains challenging with low data quality
Emerging markets and developing economies underrepresented in membership
No standardized definition of 'transition finance' across alliances

title: Social and Sustainability Bond Market Overview
description: Beyond green bonds, the labelled bond market includes social bonds (addressing social issues) and sustainability bonds (combining green and social use of proceeds). Together with sustainability-linked bonds, these form the GSS+ bond market.

greenBonds.description: Use of proceeds for environmental projects

socialBonds.description: Use of proceeds for social projects (affordable housing, healthcare, education, food security, SME financing)

sustainabilityBonds.description: Combination of green and social use of proceeds

sustainabilityLinkedBonds.description: Coupon or structure linked to achievement of sustainability KPIs; not use-of-proceeds instruments

title: ESG Ratings Coverage and Scores for GCC Corporates
description: ESG ratings coverage of GCC-listed companies has expanded significantly, though the region still has lower average scores compared to European peers due to hydrocarbon exposure and evolving disclosure practices.

spGlobalCSA.companiesAssessed: Over 150 GCC companies participate or are assessed

challenges: Governance structures (family/sovereign ownership) differ from Western models used by rating agencies
Relatively new sustainability reporting requirements (ADX ESG Guide, Saudi CMA ESG disclosure)
opportunities: Rapid improvement trajectory — GCC companies are among the fastest improvers globally
Mandated ESG disclosure in UAE (ADX) and Saudi Arabia (CMA) driving coverage expansion
Major issuers (ADNOC, Saudi Aramco, SABIC) investing heavily in ESG improvement

title: PCAF Methodology for Sovereign Debt Financed Emissions
description: PCAF extended its standard to cover sovereign debt, enabling financial institutions to measure GHG emissions associated with government bond holdings. This is critical as sovereign bonds constitute the largest fixed-income asset class globally.

methodology.attributionApproach: Production-based: attribute sovereign GHG emissions based on the financial institution's share of the sovereign's total debt
methodology.formula: Financed Emissions = (Outstanding sovereign bonds held / Total sovereign debt) * Sovereign GHG emissions
methodology.emissionsScope: Territorial (production-based) GHG emissions of the sovereign
methodology.dataSource: UNFCCC national inventories, World Bank, IEA

dataQualityScores.description: Verified national GHG inventory data (UNFCCC reporting)
Unverified national GHG inventory, or third-party estimated using national activity data
Estimated using internationally available proxy data (e.g., GDP-based emission factors)

challenges: Attribution is to the sovereign, not specific government expenditures
No mechanism to link specific bond purchases to emission-reduction activities
Double counting risk with corporate financed emissions in the same country
Government emissions influenced by policy but not solely controlled by bond proceeds

title: GCC Green and Sustainable Bond Market Development
description: The GCC green and sustainable bond market has grown rapidly from near-zero in 2018 to over USD 30 billion in cumulative issuance by 2024, driven by sovereign initiatives, national oil company diversification, and banking sector leadership.

keyDrivers: COP28 hosted in UAE (2023) catalyzed regional sustainable finance activity
Regulatory push: ADX ESG disclosure, CMA sustainability reporting
Investor demand for GCC sustainable assets

title: MSCI ESG Key Issues by Major Sector
description: MSCI assigns material ESG key issues to each GICS sub-industry. This entry covers the most material key issues and their typical weightings for major sectors, illustrating how ESG materiality varies across industries.

title: PCAF Facilitated Emissions and Insurance-Associated Emissions
description: PCAF expanded its standard to cover facilitated emissions (from capital markets activities such as underwriting and advisory) and insurance-associated emissions (from underwriting insurance policies), addressing gaps in Scope 3 Category 15 measurement.

facilitatedEmissions.definition: GHG emissions associated with capital markets transactions where the financial institution facilitates (but does not hold) the securities — e.g., underwriting equity or debt issuances, IPOs, securitizations
facilitatedEmissions.attributionMethod: Based on the financial institution's share of total facilitated amount, pro-rated over the tenor of the instrument
facilitatedEmissions.formula: Facilitated Emissions = (Share of facilitation / Total facilitated amount) * (Weighting factor based on tenor) * Issuer emissions

insuranceAssociatedEmissions.definition: GHG emissions from commercial lines insurance (re)underwriting, representing the economic activity enabled by insurance coverage
insuranceAssociatedEmissions.scope: Commercial property, energy, marine, aviation, motor, and liability insurance lines
insuranceAssociatedEmissions.attributionMethod: Based on the insurer's share of premium relative to total insured value, applied to policyholder emissions
insuranceAssociatedEmissions.formula: Insurance-associated Emissions = (Premium / Economic value of insured asset) * Policyholder emissions

data.significance: Facilitated and insurance emissions are essential for financial institutions whose primary business is capital markets or (re)insurance rather than lending
